response: Follow the breath test instructions from LabCorp; many require fasting for a period before the test.
We can send the breath test order to LabCorp electronically. Please confirm your email/phone so we send the correct order.
For accurate H. pylori breath testing, you usually need to be off PPIs/acid reducers for about 10 days beforehand.
All prescriptions, including medications needed to prepare for procedures, are sent electronically to the patient’s pharmacy through the Epic electronic medical record system.
Blood thinners are managed with the prescribing doctor (cardiology/PCP). Typical hold is 3–7 days (sometimes 2 if cleared). Please follow your prescriber’s guidance and let us know the plan.
Stop aspirin/NSAIDs now and don’t take more before the procedure. If you took a small amount recently, it’s often still okay—call us if you’re worried.
Carafate (sucralfate) is a coating medication that can help calm irritated esophagus/stomach lining. Take it as prescribed and coordinate timing with other meds.
Creon dosing is based on meal size. You can increase dose for larger meals (e.g., add 1 capsule, then 2 if needed). Follow up if symptoms persist.
If NSAIDs are needed for pain, discuss with Dr. Shelby. Sometimes acid protection (Pepcid/Carafate) is used, but avoid NSAIDs near procedure and if ulcer risk is high.
Pepcid AC can be used as a booster when symptoms break through, often at bedtime or twice daily depending on instructions.
GLP-1 meds slow stomach emptying, increasing aspiration risk with anesthesia. That’s why we typically hold them 1 week pre-procedure.
Yes—stop NSAIDs (ibuprofen/Advil/Motrin) about a week before unless your doctor tells you otherwise. Tylenol is okay.
Bile-acid binders can cause constipation. They can be used as-needed and tapered/stopped if constipation develops.
Carafate (sucralfate) dosing varies by plan. Follow your prescription label and Dr. Shelby’s guidance; timing can matter with other meds.
Celebrex is typically held about a week before procedures unless directed otherwise. Confirm with the office for your case.
We can submit a refill request, but if it’s been a long time since last visit, a consult may be required before ongoing refills.
H. pylori treatment often requires 4 medications. Start only when you have all components and follow the schedule provided.
If antibiotics cause significant diarrhea, Imodium can help short-term. If severe or persistent, stop antibiotics and call us for guidance.
If you can’t tolerate H. pylori antibiotics, Dr. Shelby may recommend a probiotic approach (e.g., Align) for a period, then repeat testing. Confirm your plan with the office.
A common guidance is holding warfarin about 3–7 days before procedure, but follow your anticoag clinic/prescriber’s exact instructions.
If you need a migraine rescue medication, ask us specifically with your med list. Many non-blood-thinner meds can be okay, but we confirm case-by-case.
If you need Creon refills or dose adjustment, email or call the office and we can help. Dose is tailored to meal size and symptoms.
If lubiprostone causes significant bloating or pain, stop it and discuss with Dr. Shelby. We may pause it around the time of prep to avoid complications.
If you have heavier dinners and nighttime reflux, Pepcid AC at bedtime can help reduce overnight symptoms.
Famotidine (Pepcid) is generally safe up to twice daily for many patients. If symptoms persist, schedule follow-up to reassess.
Pepto-Bismol can turn stools black. That can be expected, but if you have true black tarry stool or weakness/dizziness, seek urgent evaluation.
Famotidine (Pepcid) is generally gentler. Some stronger acid meds can have more long-term side effects. Dr. Shelby may recommend switching depending on your case.
Pantoprazole/Protonix is usually taken about 30 minutes before breakfast for best effect.
Short steroid tapers often do not affect a procedure date if completed beforehand. If you’re unsure, call us with your timeline.
If cholestyramine/bile-acid binder causes constipation, reduce frequency or stop and use it as-needed.
If PPIs cause side effects, Dr. Shelby may switch you to Pepcid and/or add Carafate. Please schedule follow-up before making major changes.
Tylenol (acetaminophen) is generally okay to use for pain before the procedure.
Cephalexin (Keflex) for UTI is generally okay prior to colonoscopy/endoscopy. If you have fever or feel very ill, call us.
A UTI on antibiotics usually does not prevent colonoscopy. If you have fever, feel very ill, or have kidney/back pain, call us.
If you’re stable on antibiotics and feel okay, you can usually keep your scheduled procedure. If you worsen (fever, vomiting, severe pain), call us.
Most antibiotics are okay prior to procedure. If you develop fever or feel significantly unwell, call us. Otherwise you can usually proceed.
Warfarin hold length should be coordinated with the anticoag clinic/prescriber. We document the plan and proceed based on prescriber clearance.
GLP-1 injections are typically held 1 week before colonoscopy to reduce aspiration risk. Follow your instructions and call if you’re unsure about timing.
The H. pylori breath test is typically done about 2 weeks after you finish antibiotics. You usually need to be off acid reducers for about 10 days beforehand.
If your biopsy shows H. pylori, we prescribe a multi-medication antibiotic regimen. After finishing, we usually do a breath test to confirm it’s gone.
If stool elastase suggests pancreatic insufficiency, Dr. Shelby may recommend pancreatic enzymes (e.g., Creon) and titrate dose based on meal size.
For nighttime reflux, a Pepcid AC at bedtime can help reduce overnight symptoms. Continue lifestyle measures (no late meals, elevation if needed).
Pepcid (famotidine) is gentle and can often be taken up to twice daily. If symptoms persist, schedule follow-up—there may be another cause.

Pantoprazole/Protonix can cause headaches in some patients. Dr. Shelby may switch you to Pepcid (famotidine) and/or add Carafate as a gentler alternative—please schedule a follow-up to adjust meds safely.
Alcohol can worsen pancreatic enzyme function. Reducing alcohol and optimizing enzyme dosing can improve stool consistency.
If you must take a critical medication (like thyroid meds), it’s often allowed with a small sip of water—otherwise take meds after the procedure. If unsure, call us.
Topical anti-inflammatory creams (like Voltaren) are usually okay. Oral NSAIDs should be held as instructed.
Most antibiotics (including for a UTI) are okay to take around the time of your procedure. If you have fever or feel very ill, call us.
A UTI and common antibiotics (like cephalexin) usually do not prevent a colonoscopy/endoscopy. If you have fever or feel very unwell, call us.
We mainly avoid high-dose vitamin E by itself before procedures. A standard multivitamin that contains vitamin E is usually okay—stop it now and you should be fine.
If your pharmacy is out of stock, we can transfer or resend to another location. Tell us the preferred pharmacy address and we’ll help reroute.
We can reroute prescriptions to a different pharmacy if needed (CVS/Walgreens/Costco/Safeway/Alto). Tell us the pharmacy name and location.
